items.title: Default hidden-layer activation
items.avoid: Using sigmoid or tanh in every hidden layer of a deep CNN without a specific reason.
items.prefer: Use ReLU (or Leaky ReLU) after conv and linear hidden layers unless the architecture doc specifies otherwise.
items.why: ReLU is the empirically validated default for vision and many MLP pipelines—faster and less saturation-prone than sigmoid.

items.title: Mitigating dying ReLU
items.avoid: Training for hundreds of epochs while many neurons never fire, hoping capacity will recover on its own.
items.prefer: Monitor dead-neuron fraction; switch to Leaky ReLU (α ≈ 0.01), use He initialization, and tune learning rate.
items.why: Zero gradient on the negative branch is self-reinforcing—dead units rarely recover without architectural or hyperparameter changes.
items.example: If `dead_neurons` list is non-empty after epoch 1, replace `nn.ReLU()` with `nn.LeakyReLU(0.01)` before epoch 5.

items.title: Output layer activation choice
items.avoid: Stacking ReLU before softmax or regression outputs.
items.prefer: Keep the final layer linear for logits (classification) or direct regression targets; apply softmax/sigmoid only where the loss expects it.
items.why: Output activations must match the loss and label space—ReLU is for hidden nonlinearity, not final predictions.
items.example: Binary classifier: `Linear → Sigmoid` or `Linear` + `BCEWithLogitsLoss`; not `Linear → ReLU → Sigmoid`.

items.title: Choosing between Leaky ReLU and PReLU
items.avoid: Defaulting to PReLU everywhere for marginal gains on small models.
items.prefer: Start with standard or Leaky ReLU; adopt PReLU when experiments show channel-specific negative slopes help and extra parameters are affordable.
items.why: PReLU adds learnable parameters and optimization surface; Leaky ReLU is simpler and often sufficient.
items.example: ResNet-style CNN: ReLU is standard; PReLU is optional ablation when chasing small accuracy gains on segmentation.
